title: Variational Quantum Algorithms

theory.introduction: Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) are hybrid quantum-classical algorithms designed to run on near-term noisy quantum computers. They use a parameterized quantum circuit (ansatz) to prepare a quantum state, measure its properties, and then use a classical optimizer to tune the parameters and minimize a cost function.
key_points: Robust to certain types of quantum noise
Foundation for near-term quantum applications like VQE and QAOA

ansatz.description: A parameterized quantum circuit that explores a specific region of the Hilbert space.

cost_function.description: A mathematical function evaluated on the quantum computer that the classical optimizer tries to minimize.

classical_optimizer.description: Classical algorithm (like COBYLA, Adam, or SPSA) that updates the parameters based on the measured cost function.

mathematical_foundation.description: The quantum computer evaluates the expectation value of an observable (like a Hamiltonian $H$), and the classical computer computes gradients to update $\theta$.

VQE.name: Variational Quantum Eigensolver
VQE.description: Used to find the ground state energy of a molecular Hamiltonian.

QAOA.name: Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm
QAOA.description: Used to solve combinatorial optimization problems like MaxCut by alternating problem and mixer Hamiltonians.

advantages: Requires shallower quantum circuits than fault-tolerant algorithms
Naturally mitigates some systemic hardware errors
Can be adapted to specific hardware topologies
challenges: Barren plateaus (vanishing gradients in large circuits)
High number of measurements required to estimate expectation values
Classical optimizer can get stuck in local minima
summary: Variational Quantum Algorithms bridge the gap to practical quantum computing. By outsourcing the heavy optimization logic to a classical computer and using the quantum computer merely to evaluate complex states, VQAs offer the most promising path to near-term quantum advantage.
